Claude Code Routines: How to Set Up /schedule (and Why It Pushes to claude/ Instead of main)
Claude Code has three ways to schedule work: Routines (cloud-based, runs even with your computer off), Desktop scheduled tasks (local, app needs to be open), and
(session-level, terminal needs to stay open). The
CLI is the entry point for Routines — minimum 1-hour interval, and
by default it only pushes to
. To push to main, flip "Allow unrestricted branch pushes" on the web side.

Three ways to schedule in Claude Code
, here's a quick comparison of the three scheduling options Claude Code offers:
With that out of the way, the rest of this post focuses on Routines (Cloud).

In short, a Routine is a prompt plus the repo it should work in (and a few other settings), parked on Anthropic's cloud. It fires on whatever schedule or trigger you set up, and runs your task on its own.
Two main things separate it from a regular Claude Code session:
Not on your computer — so it still fires when you're asleep, when you've gone out, or when your laptop's off.
No permission prompts (no Yes/No popups), it just follows your prompt all the way through. Which means the prompt has to be really clear — otherwise it might do something you didn't expect.

If you want to squeeze the most out of your monthly subscription, Routines are actually pretty useful for recurring tasks — checking for new PRs to review daily, summarizing the week's commit log on Fridays, opening a monthly PR to update the stats chart in your README, that kind of thing.
Routines currently support three trigger types:

Either works — they both edit the same record, so creating or editing on one side syncs to the other instantly.
language-bash shiki shiki-themes github-light github-light github-dark-high-contrast
with nothing after it, and Claude walks you through it conversationally — asking what the prompt should be, which repo, how often it should run, etc.
If you already know exactly what you want, you can write it inline:
/schedule in 2 weeks, open a cleanup PR that removes the feature flag
Claude parses the time you wrote, converts it to a concrete timestamp, confirms with you, and saves it.

OK now the part that confused me — the "I asked for main but it pushed to
When a Routine runs a prompt that pushes code,
. So even if your prompt says "push directly to main when done", Claude opens something like
and pushes there. Your actual main stays untouched.

This isn't a bug — it's a feature (really). The reasoning is to "stop routines from accidentally messing up important branches". Scheduled tasks run autonomously, nobody's watching, and there are no permission prompts. If they could write straight to main, one badly written prompt could blow up a production project.
So how do you handle it? Two options:

, then open a PR for review. This is the recommended workflow — since nobody's watching when the routine runs, having changes land on a separate branch first and merging when you have time is just a lot safer.

If you really want it to push to main directly, you can flip this in the Permissions section when editing the routine. No more branch prefix restriction — but you'd better make sure the prompt is rock-solid and won't do anything you don't want.
If you really do want it to push straight to a specific branch, here's how:
Hit the pencil icon to enter Edit routine
Find the Permissions section at the bottom
Where to find Allow unrestricted branch pushes

If your main is protected with a branch protection rule, GitHub will still block the push even with "Allow unrestricted branch pushes" turned on. At that point you either add an exception to the protection rule, or just go through the PR flow — which is probably more practical anyway.
Where it runs and what it can access
Besides the wrong-branch thing, there are a couple of Routine details that are easy to miss the first time you set one up.
Every run is a fresh clone of the default branch
Every time a Routine fires, it
clones a fresh copy of the default branch from your chosen repo
— it doesn't carry over a working tree from the last run. So you can't write a schedule that "continues yesterday's half-finished work". Every run starts from scratch.
If your task needs to reference past state, write that state into a file in the repo, or hook up to a third-party service.
One-off schedules don't count against the daily cap
Routines have a daily run cap (the max number of runs per day), but
one-off schedules don't count against that cap
— they just draw from your subscription's general usage. So if you want to use scheduling as a "remind me in two weeks" tool, you can stack a bunch of these without burning through the daily cap.
Two ways to manage existing routines.
On the web side, go to
, click into a routine's detail page, and you get more:
— fire it immediately without waiting for the next slot
— pause or resume the schedule; pausing doesn't delete the routine itself
— name, prompt, repo, environment, Connectors, trigger type
— removes the routine; past run sessions stay in your session list

Speaking of which — every time a routine runs, a new session shows up in your session list. You can click in, see what Claude did, review changes, open a PR, or just keep the conversation going from where the routine left off.
Claude Code's scheduling feature isn't all that complicated once you get it — the main things are: know the differences between the three scheduling types, learn how
. After that it's just tuning trigger types and environment settings to match your needs.

My own habit at this point — daily "run this small thing every day" goes into Routines as a daily schedule, batch tasks that need local file access become Desktop scheduled tasks, and anything I want to poll repeatedly inside a current session uses
. They each have their own slot.
" thing — once I knew it was intentional design, I made my peace with it. Letting it push to
and reviewing through a PR is honestly more reassuring anyway. If you've been hitting the same confusion, hopefully this saved you a trip into the docs.

Why does it push to claude/xxx when I asked for main?

I scheduled a task with Claude Code's /schedule, came back to the repo next day, and asked myself: I told it to push to main, why are there a bunch of claude/xxx branches instead? This post walks through Claude Code's three scheduling options (Routines, Desktop scheduled tasks, /loop), how /schedule works on the CLI, Routines' three trigger types (Schedule, API, GitHub event), custom cron rules, the 1-hour minimum interval — and most importantly, why pushes land on claude/-prefixed branches and how to flip "Allow unrestricted branch pushes" to fix that confusing behavior.
